steps.title: Tools Python gives you for free

narration.default: Python comes with dozens of small functions ready to use — no import needed. Today we'll meet the most useful ones for working with lists: sum, max, min, len, sorted, type. They each do one specific job, and together they're enough to write a real little stats program in five lines.

Type:

numbers = [5, 8, 2, 10]

print("Numbers:", numbers)

The square brackets here create a LIST — a single name that holds many values in order. numbers holds five-eight-two-ten as one bundle. Lists are everywhere in Python: shopping items, quiz questions, scores, friend names. The first print shows the whole list.

steps.title: sum, max, min — three at once

narration.default:
Type three more lines:

print("Total:", sum(numbers))
print("Highest:", max(numbers))
print("Lowest:", min(numbers))

sum adds every number in the list. max returns the biggest, min the smallest. All three are standard Python — they work on any list of numbers, no matter how long. Imagine writing a for loop yourself for each — these save you the work.

Type:

print("Count:", len(numbers))

len tells you how many things are in a list. Here it'll print 4, because the list has four numbers. len also works on strings — len("Python") is 6 — and on most other Python collections.

steps.title: sorted — make a new ordered list

narration.default:
Type:

print("Sorted:", sorted(numbers))

sorted gives you back a NEW list, smallest to largest, without changing the original. After this line numbers is still [5, 8, 2, 10] — the print just shows [2, 5, 8, 10]. Important: sorted does not modify; it returns a new sorted copy.

steps.title: type — what KIND of value is this?

narration.default:
Type the last line:

print("Type:", type(numbers))

type shows what KIND of thing a value is — Python prints something like <class 'list'>. Useful when debugging — if a print shows a string when you expected a number, type tells you so. type works on any value: type(5) is int, type("hi") is str, type(True) is bool.
Click Run. The terminal shows six lines: the original list, the total 25, the highest 10, the lowest 2, the count 4, the sorted version, and the type. Six built-in tools, one short program.

Change numbers to your test scores, your daily steps, the number of likes on your last 5 posts. The same six tools work on any list. Pair them with strings, files, scores, votes — anywhere you have a bundle of values, these built-ins save you a loop.
